summary: Joel Spolsky offers seven pieces of career advice for computer science college students, drawing from his own experience and observations about what makes programmers successful. He emphasizes that writing skills, knowledge of C, and microeconomics are essential foundations that most CS programs neglect. He argues that GPA matters because it reflects consistent effort across boring and interesting work alike, and that practical programming-intensive courses are more valuable than pure theory. He dismisses fears about offshoring and urges students to build strong resumes through programming internships rather than wasting summers on non-technical jobs. The overarching message is that communication skills and business understanding differentiate great programmers from merely tolerable ones.
key_points: Writing ability is the single biggest differentiator between tolerable and great programmers, because it enables leverage through persuasion, documentation, and specifications
Learning C is essential not because you'll use it daily, but because understanding the machine prevents you from programming based on superstition
Microeconomics teaches the business fundamentals that make a programmer more valuable — programmers with crazy ideas that make sense in code but not in capitalism are frustrating to work with
GPA matters because it reflects sustained effort across many subjects, including boring ones — employers want people who perform consistently, not just when the work is fun
Computer science is a different subject than software development, and students should seek out programming-intensive practicum courses rather than pure theory
money_quotes: The difference between a tolerable programmer and a great programmer is not how many programming languages they know, and it's not whether they prefer Python or Java. It's whether they can communicate their ideas.
If you enjoy programming computers, count your blessings: you are in a very fortunate minority of people who can make a great living doing work they enjoy.

There's a lot of wonderful, useful code buried on sourceforge somewhere that nobody uses because it was created by programmers who don't write very well (or don't write at all), and so nobody knows what they've done and their brilliant code languishes.
Oh, so, you're saying I should hire you because you don't work very hard when the work is boring? Well, there's boring stuff in programming, too.
If you can't explain why while (*s++ = *t++); copies a string, or if that isn't the most natural thing in the world to you, well, you're programming based on superstition.
Computer science is not the same as software development. If you're really really lucky, your school might have a decent software development curriculum.
A programmer who understands the fundamentals of business is going to be a more valuable programmer, to a business, than a programmer who doesn't. That's all there is to it.
key_insight: What separates great programmers from mediocre ones isn't technical depth but the ability to write clearly, understand business fundamentals, and perform consistently even when the work is boring.
